experiences.intentions:
Player 2: I am Merlin and know 1 and 3 are evil. The proposed team [0,1] includes known evil player 1, so as Good I should reject to avoid an immediate sabotage opportunity. Rejecting also avoids signaling too much beyond a standard anti-suspicious-team vote.
Player 3: I know player 1 is evil with me, so this proposed team contains one evil and one unknown. On round 1 as evil, approving a team with an evil teammate is strong because it gives us a chance to fail quest 1 immediately and creates ambiguity around player 0 and 1. My vote itself reveals little; approving is consistent with many alignments and helps evil maximize chance of an early failed quest.
Player 0: I have no role-specific knowledge beyond being Good, and there is no behavioral info yet since this is the first proposal. As a Servant on round 1, approving a reasonable opening team is standard to generate quest information rather than risking needless early rejections. My vote reveals little beyond a normal willingness to test an initial team, which helps Good collect information from the quest result.
Player 4: No concrete role information yet from quest outcomes or voting patterns. As a Good Servant on round 1 quest 1, a common optimal policy is to approve the first 2-person team absent strong evidence, since early rejection gives little information and this team could easily be all-good. My vote reveals little beyond a standard cooperative opening and does not unduly help Evil.
Player 1: I know Player 3 is my evil teammate; players 0, 2, and 4 are good. Proposed team [0,1] includes me and a good player. As evil on the first vote, approving is strong because if it passes I can choose to fail quest 1 and put immediate pressure on good, while an approve vote on an early plausible team is not very revealing. Rejecting my own inclusion would look odd and gives up a chance to score an early fail.

experiences.intentions:
Player 0: I am a Good Servant on the quest team, so I must vote pass. Information is limited: player 2 rejected the opening 0/1 team while others approved, which is a mild data point but not enough to infer roles. In quest voting, optimal good play is always pass; failing would falsely sabotage my own team and give evil an undeserved point. My action reveals no extra hidden info beyond standard good behavior and helps the Good side maximize success chances on quest 1.
Player 1: I know player 3 is my evil teammate; players 0,2,4 are good or Merlin/Percival. On quest 1 with a 2-person team [0,1], an early fail would immediately place suspicion on me because I am the only known overlap from my perspective and evil often prefers to avoid obvious exposure on the first quest in 5-player Avalon. Optimal evil play here is usually to pass and preserve cover for both evil players, especially since there are many future opportunities to sabotage. Passing reveals less information and helps me remain trusted for later teams, which benefits evil more than an immediate fail.

experiences.intentions:
Player 4: Quest 1 team [0,1] succeeded, so 0 and 1 are somewhat more likely good, though evil could hide with a pass. As a Servant, I want to approve reasonable low-risk teams and avoid needless rejections early. Adding 2 to a previously successful pair is acceptable and the vote itself reveals limited info; rejecting without stronger evidence gives little benefit.
Player 0: Quest 1 succeeded with team [0,1], so 0 and 1 look somewhat better, though a 2-player success does not clear both. Adding 2 to make [0,1,2] is fairly reasonable for quest 2 since it preserves the prior successful core. As a good Servant, I want to approve plausible information-gathering teams, especially early when there is no strong evidence against 2. Approving reveals I support continuity from the successful team, which helps good more than it helps evil here.
Player 2: I am Merlin and know 1 and 3 are evil. Proposed team [0,1,2] includes known evil player 1, so it should be rejected. As Good/Merlin, optimal play is to avoid approving teams with confirmed evil while not revealing my knowledge too explicitly. Rejecting helps deny evil access to the quest and is consistent with normal good play after a suspicious prior success on [0,1], which likely hid evil. My vote itself reveals little because many good players would also reject a team containing someone from the previous ambiguous team plus a new member.
Player 1: I am evil Assassin with teammate 3. Team [0,1,2] includes me but not teammate 3. Previous team [0,1] succeeded with 0 fails; since I was on it, I likely passed to avoid suspicion, making 0 and me look good. As leader proposing this team, approving is optimal because it keeps me trusted and places one evil on a 3-person quest where I can decide later whether to fail based on table state. Rejecting my own team would be suspicious and gives no benefit. Approving reveals consistency and helps evil maintain cover.
Player 3: I know Player 1 is evil with me. The previous team [0,1] succeeded despite including 1, so 1 likely passed to avoid exposure. Current team [0,1,2] again includes my teammate and not me. As evil, I want to preserve flexibility and avoid making an obviously coordinated evil reject on a plausible team. Approving keeps 1 on the quest, where evil can choose whether to fail based on broader deception needs. My vote reveals little because approving a reasonable 3-person team after a successful first quest is normal.

experiences.intentions:
Player 2: I am Merlin and know 1 and 3 are evil. Team [0,2] excludes both known evil players, so it is the strongest available 2-player team. Previous fail on [0,1,2] is fully explained by player 1 being evil, and previous success on [0,1] can happen if 1 chose to pass. As good, I should approve strong clean teams while not revealing too much; approving this is natural and helps good.
Player 1: I know 3 is my evil teammate, so 0/2/4 are good. Quest 2 with team [0,1,2] failed from my single fail, which makes me somewhat suspicious if people track proposals, but [0,2] is all good and should succeed. As evil in team voting, I want to reject clean good teams when possible to push toward worse compositions later. Rejecting reveals mild opposition to a strong-looking team, but that is acceptable since evil generally benefits from blocking good-only teams.
Player 0: Quest 1 with [0,1] succeeded, so 0/1 looked okay then. Quest 2 with [0,1,2] had exactly one fail, so at least one of {0,1,2} is evil. Since I know I am Good, at least one of {1,2} is evil. Proposed team [0,2] excludes 1 but keeps 2; if 2 is the evil from quest 2, this 2-player quest will fail. As Good servant on team voting, optimal play is to reject teams that are not strongly good. Approving would reveal little and risks handing Evil another fail. Rejecting pressures leader rotation and more info.
Player 4: Quest 1 with [0,1] succeeded, so at least one of 0/1 is likely good. Quest 2 with [0,1,2] had exactly one fail, so exactly one of 0/1/2 is evil. Team [0,2] excludes 1, who is currently the strongest good candidate because replacing 2 with 1 would avoid the known fail if 2 is evil, and if 0 were evil then [0,2] is still risky. As a good servant on team voting, I should reject teams that are not the best evidence-based option. Rejecting also signals preference for testing [0,1] or [1,2] rather than blindly trusting 0 and 2 together.
Player 3: I know player 1 is evil with me. Quest 1 on [0,1] succeeded, likely because 1 chose to pass to avoid early exposure. Quest 2 on [0,1,2] failed with exactly one fail; since 1 is evil, that fail could easily have come from 1, which keeps suspicion on both 1 and 2/0 somewhat diffuse. The current proposed team [0,2] excludes both known evils, so from an evil perspective this team is dangerous because it is likely all-good and would give Good a second success. In team voting, optimal evil play is usually to reject clean teams, especially when they exclude all evils and there is no auto-pass pressure yet (round 1 of 5). My reject slightly signals resistance to a clean team, but that is acceptable; evil generally wants this team blocked.

experiences.intentions:
Player 2: I am Merlin and know players 1 and 3 are evil. The proposed team is exactly [1,3], two known evil players, so approving would guarantee evil control of the quest and likely another fail. In team voting, optimal good play is to reject clearly bad teams. My reject vote signals opposition to an obviously suspicious team, which helps good more than it hurts; as Merlin I should still avoid overexplaining, but the action itself is straightforward.
Player 4: I am Good Servant. Team [0,1] succeeded on quest 1, while [0,1,2] failed on quest 2 with exactly one fail, so at least one of {0,1,2} is evil. Since [0,1] was clean, player 2 is the strongest evil candidate. Player 3 rejected [0,2], which is slightly good-looking, and now proposes [1,3], excluding the main suspect 2. This team is plausible, though 1 could still be evil. As Good, on round 2 I should approve a team that sensibly avoids the likeliest evil rather than risk cycling. Approving also tests 3 and gives information on 1.
Player 1: I know player 3 is my evil teammate, and I was on the failed quest [0,1,2], so at least one of us on that team is evil from others' perspective; since I am evil, team [1,3] contains two evils and can guarantee a fail. In team voting, the optimal evil play is to approve a team with both evil players because it advances a likely failed quest while looking somewhat plausible after prior suspicion on me. Approving reveals little beyond willingness to back my own proposed-looking evil-leaning team, and the strategic gain of securing two evils on quest outweighs any suspicion.
Player 0: Quest 1 with [0,1] succeeded, so 0/1 looked good then. Quest 2 with [0,1,2] had exactly one fail, so at least one of 0/1/2 is evil. As I know I am good, that means either 1 or 2 is evil. Player 2 then proposed [0,2] and was heavily rejected, which adds some suspicion to 2, but not conclusive. Current proposed team [1,3] excludes me and includes 1, who is in the {1,2} suspect pair; 3 is largely untested. As a good servant, I should reject teams that include a likely evil when better information-preserving options exist. Approving a 2-person team with 1 suspect is risky and gives little guaranteed value. Rejecting signals concern about 1 and pushes for a safer lineup involving me plus less-conflicted players.
Player 3: I am Minion with evil teammate 1. Team [1,3] contains both evil players, so if approved we can force a fail on quest 3. Prior teams: [0,1] succeeded, [0,1,2] failed with exactly one fail, which is consistent with 1 failing and may make 1 somewhat suspicious, but pairing 1 with me could also look like an evil stack. Still, as evil on team voting, I should approve any team containing enough evil to control the quest, especially both evils. My approve vote supports a highly favorable team for evil. The information revealed by approving is limited since evil often approves strong evil teams and we need this team to pass.

experiences.intentions:
Player 4: We know quest 1 team [0,1,2] had exactly 1 evil, and quest 3 team [1,3] had 2 fail votes so both 1 and 3 are evil. Therefore 0 and 2 are good from quest 1. As player 4 good, the proposed team [0,2,4] is all good and should be approved. Approving also helps lock in the correct final quest team with maximum confidence.
Player 2: I am Merlin and know 1 and 3 are evil, so the proposed team [0,2,4] excludes both known evil players and is the strongest possible 3-person team from my perspective. On quest 4 with evil leading 2-1, approving a clean team is critical. My approve vote supports a likely-successful quest; while my pattern of supporting good teams could slightly signal information, rejecting would be much worse for Good here.
Player 0: Quest 1 [0,1,2] had exactly 1 fail, so among 0/1/2 there is exactly one evil; since I know I am good, exactly one of 1 or 2 is evil. Quest 2 [1,3] had 2 fails, so both 1 and 3 are evil. Therefore 2 is good, and player 4 is the remaining good. Team [0,2,4] is all good. As good on team voting, I should approve the clean team to stop evil from winning quest 4. Approving also signals confidence in the solved reads and helps good coordinate.
Player 1: I know player 3 is my evil teammate. Quest 3 team [1,3] produced 2 fails, so both 1 and 3 are exposed as evil to any attentive players. The proposed team [0,2,4] excludes both known evils and on 5-player Avalon quest 4 still needs only 1 fail to fail, so this team is likely all-good and would probably succeed, bringing the score to 2-2 and giving good momentum. As evil, optimal play is to reject strong good teams whenever possible. My vote reveals little since I’m already suspicious from being on a double-fail team; rejecting a team without either exposed evil is consistent with evil interests.
Player 3: I know player 1 is evil with me. Quest 1 on [0,1,2] had exactly one fail, so either 1 or I? I wasn't on it, so the fail came from 1, confirming 0 and 2 could be good. Quest 3 on [1,3] had two fails, confirming both 1 and I are evil. Proposed team [0,2,4] excludes both known evils, which is very likely an all-good team and would succeed, putting good at 2 successes and threatening evil's lead. As evil in team voting, I should reject strong good teams. My reject also provides little extra info because evil is expected to oppose clean teams at this stage.

experiences.intentions:
Player 2: I am Merlin and know 1 and 3 are evil, so 0,2,4 is the only all-good 3-person team. On the final 3-person quest, approving the clean team maximizes good's chance to win. Approving is also consistent with prior successful result on [0,2,4] and does not reveal extra information beyond supporting the strongest good team.
Player 3: I know Player 1 is my evil teammate. Team [0,2,4] excludes both evil players, and it previously succeeded with 0 fails, strongly suggesting it is all good. In team voting on quest 5 at 2-2, evil wants to prevent a likely good success team from going. Rejecting is optimal to try to force a different team later. My vote also aligns with an evil preference against clean good teams, though one vote alone may not stop it.
Player 4: I am Good Servant. Known data: [1,3] had 2 fails, so both 1 and 3 are Evil. Team [0,2,4] previously succeeded with 0 fails, making 0 and 2 very likely Good alongside me. On quest 5, the proposed team [0,2,4] excludes the two confirmed Evils and is the best possible 3-person team. Approving reveals consistent Good reasoning and helps my team win; rejecting only risks unnecessary delay.
Player 0: I’m Good with limited info. Team [0,2,4] previously succeeded with 0 fails, which is our strongest evidence for a clean 3-player team. The only confirmed double-fail was [1,3], so 1 and 3 are highly suspicious as the two Evil players. On the deciding 3-person quest, approving the previously successful team is optimal. Voting reject only risks cycling into worse teams and gives no extra useful information. My vote signals trust in the best-evidenced team, which helps Good more than it helps Evil.
Player 1: I am evil Assassin with teammate 3. Proposed team is 0,2,4 and excludes both evil players, so if approved the final quest would almost certainly succeed, giving Good the game immediately at 3 successes. As evil, I should reject any all-good team on quest 5. Public info: team [0,2,4] previously succeeded with 0 fails, which strongly suggests they may all be good; rejecting aligns with preventing a guaranteed good win. My vote reveals some opposition to this team, but preserving evil chances matters more.
